content:
# Genesis Chapter 3 -- The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 2v-3r | **Source Image:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_003_page_001.jpg, GEN_003_page_002.jpg
**Additional Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209) — LXX | **View Vaticanus online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209

---

**3:1** And-the-serpent was [the] *'arum* [cunning/shrewd] from-all [the]-beast of-the-field which made YHWH God. And-he-said to the-woman, "Indeed, [is it true] that said God, 'Not you-shall-eat from-every tree of-the-garden'?"

**3:2** And-said the-woman to the-serpent, "From [the]-fruit of [the]-tree of-the-garden we-may-eat.

**3:3** But-from [the]-fruit of-the-tree which [is] in-[the]-midst of-the-garden, God said, 'Not you-shall-eat from-it and-not you-shall-touch it, lest you-die.'"

**3:4** And-said the-serpent to the-woman, "Not dying you-shall-die.

**3:5** For [the] knowing of God, that in-the-day of-your-eating from-it, and-will-be-opened your-eyes, and-you-shall-be like God, knowing good and-evil."

**3:6** And-saw the-woman that good [was] the-tree for-food, and-that [it was] a-desire to-the-eyes, and-desirable the-tree to-make-[one]-wise, and-she-took from-its-fruit and-she-ate, and-she-gave also to-her-man with-her, and-he-ate.

**3:7** And-were-opened the-eyes of-[the]-two-of-them, and-they-knew that naked they-[were]. And-they-sewed leaves of-fig-tree and-they-made for-themselves loin-coverings [*chagorot*].

**3:8** And-they-heard [the]-sound of YHWH God walking in-the-garden in-[the]-breeze of-the-day, and-hid-himself the-man and-his-woman from-before YHWH God in-[the]-midst of [the]-tree of-the-garden.

**3:9** And-called YHWH God to the-man, and-said to-him, "Where-are-you [*'ayyekkah*]?"

**3:10** And-said, "Your-sound I-heard in-the-garden, and-I-feared, for naked I [am], and-I-hid-myself."

**3:11** And-said, "Who told-you that naked you [are]? From-the-tree which I-commanded you not to-eat from-it, [have] you-eaten?"

**3:12** And-said the-man, "The-woman whom You-gave with-me, she gave to-me from-the-tree, and-I-ate."

**3:13** And-said YHWH God to the-woman, "What [is] this you-have-done?" And-said the-woman, "The-serpent deceived me, and-I-ate."

**3:14** And-said YHWH God to the-serpent, "Because you-did this, cursed you [are] from-all the-cattle and-from-all [the]-beast of-the-field; on your-belly you-shall-go, and-dust you-shall-eat all [the]-days of-your-life.

**3:15** And-enmity I-will-put between-you and-between the-woman, and-between your-seed and-between her-seed; he will-bruise you [the]-head and-you will-bruise him [the]-heel."

**3:16** To the-woman He-said, "Multiplying I-will-multiply your-pain and-your-conception; in-pain you-shall-bring-forth children. And-to your-man [shall be] your-desire, and-he shall-rule over-you."

**3:17** And-to Adam He-said, "Because you-listened to [the]-voice of your-woman, and-you-ate from the-tree which I-commanded you, saying, 'Not you-shall-eat from-it'; cursed [is] the-ground on-your-account; in-pain you-shall-eat it all [the]-days of-your-life.

**3:18** And-thorns and-thistles it-shall-sprout-up for-you, and-you-shall-eat [the]-herb of-the-field.

**3:19** By-[the]-sweat of your-nostrils [face] you-shall-eat bread, until your-return to the-ground, for from-it you-were-taken, for dust you [are], and-to-dust you-shall-return."

**3:20** And-called the-man [the]-name of his-woman Chavvah [Eve], for she was [the]-mother of all living.

**3:21** And-made YHWH God for Adam and-for his-woman garments of skin, and-He-clothed them.

**3:22** And-said YHWH God, "Behold, the-man has-become like one of-us, to-know good and-evil. And-now, lest he-stretch-out his-hand, and-take also from [the]-tree of-the-life, and-eat, and-live to-eternity—"

**3:23** And-sent-him-out YHWH God from [the]-garden of Eden, to-work the-ground from-which he-was-taken.

**3:24** And-He-drove-out the-man, and-He-caused-to-dwell from-east [*miqqedem*] to [the]-garden of Eden the-cherubim and-[the]-flame of [the]-sword the-revolving, to-guard [the]-way of [the]-tree of-the-life.

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

### Prefatory Remarks

Genesis 3 is among the most linguistically and theologically dense passages in the Hebrew Bible. It is translated here directly from the Leningrad Codex (B19A), folios 2v–3r, as photographed by the West Semitic Research Project (images credited: Bruce E. Zuckerman). The manuscript pages have been examined directly: the three-column layout of square Ashkenazic-influenced Masoretic script with full Tiberian vocalization (niqqud) and cantillation marks (te'amim) is visible throughout. The Masoretic marginal notes (Masorah Parva, in small script at the column edges, and Masorah Magna, at the top and bottom of the page) are also visible in the images, confirming the standard Ben Asher textual tradition. The chapter opens on folio 2v (page image 1, right column) immediately following Gen 2:25, and concludes on folio 3r (page image 2) before the beginning of Gen 4. Additional LXX readings are drawn from Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), page image GEN_003_vaticanus_page_001.jpg — the heading "CAP. III" is visible at the top of the page in a later hand; the uncial text runs continuously in three columns. DSS evidence is drawn from 4QGen-b (4Q2), which preserves fragments of Genesis 3, assessed against the DJD series vol. XII (Ulrich et al.).

Truth before beauty. — EMET

---

### Note 1 — 3:1: The *'Arum*/*'Arummim* Wordplay (Critical Connection to 2:25)

**Hebrew:** וְהַנָּחָשׁ הָיָה עָרוּם מִכֹּל חַיַּת הַשָּׂדֶה

The chapter's opening word-play is the most important single literary feature connecting chapter 3 to chapter 2, and it is invisible in most translations. Genesis 2:25 closes with: "and-they-were naked [*'arummim*, עֲרוּמִּים], the-man and-his-woman, and-not they-were-ashamed." Genesis 3:1 opens immediately with: "and-the-serpent was *'arum* [עָרוּם], cunning/shrewd."

The two words share identical consonants: ע–ר–ם (*'-r-m*). The difference is entirely in the vowel pattern and morphology: *'arummim* (3ms plural adjective, Qere *'arummim* / Ketiv may be the same) means "naked," while *'arum* (singular adjective from a denominative of *'ormah*, cunning) means "shrewd, crafty, prudent." In the Leningrad Codex, the word is written עָרוּם in 3:1 with a *qamets* under the *'ayin* and a *shureq* after the *resh*, clearly distinct from עֲרוּמִּים in 2:25 with a *hateph-patah* and doubled *mem*.

The literary effect is a jarring juxtaposition: the reader sees "naked" (2:25) and immediately "cunning" (3:1), hearing the same consonants, creating an echo of innocence/shrewdness. The couple is *naked* — exposed, innocent, unguarded; the serpent is *'arum* — armed with cunning, the very quality the couple lacks. The wordplay implies that the serpent will exploit precisely what the couple is missing.

This wordplay is untranslatable in English without artifice. I have preserved it by transliterating *'arum* with a bracketed gloss "[cunning/shrewd]" in 3:1, and by referencing the *'arummim* of 2:25. — EMET

---

### Note 2 — 3:1: *Af ki* (אַף כִּי) — The Serpent's Opening Gambit

The serpent's opening word is אַף כִּי — *'af ki* — variously rendered "indeed," "really," "even if," "has God truly said?" The construction is an intensifying particle (*'af*) plus a subordinating conjunction (*ki*). The effect is one of feigned surprise, casting doubt by appearing to merely ask a question.

The serpent does not say "God did not say that." It says something more insidious: it misquotes God's actual command ("not you-shall-eat from *every* tree") when in fact God said "from *every* tree you-may-eat, but *from one* you shall not" (2:16–17). The serpent distorts by exaggeration, making the prohibition sound total rather than singular. This misquotation is deliberate — it forces the woman to correct the record, drawing her into the very conversation. I have not smoothed this syntax.

The LXX renders *'af ki* as ὅτι εἶπεν ὁ θεός — "because/that God said" — which is significantly flatter than the Hebrew and loses the sharp ironic edge of *'af ki*. The Vaticanus reading confirms this flattening. The MT's rhetorical sharpness is to be preserved. — EMET

---

### Note 3 — 3:1: The Serpent — *Nachash* (נָחָשׁ)

The Hebrew *nachash* (נָחָשׁ) means "serpent" or "snake." The word is also related to the verb *nichesh* (to divine, to practice divination) and the noun *nechoshet* (bronze/copper). The identification of this creature with "the satan" of later Jewish tradition or the "devil" of Christian theology is a post-textual development; the Leningrad Codex presents a creature (*chayyat ha-sadeh*, "beast of the field") — not a supernatural being. The text says only that YHWH God made it (3:1) and that it is more *'arum* (cunning) than other creatures.

The LXX Vaticanus renders *nachash* as ὄφις (*ophis*, "serpent"), the standard Greek word. No variant in identification. — EMET

---

### Note 4 — 3:3: The Woman's Addition — "And You Shall Not Touch It"

YHWH God's command in 2:17 was: "you-shall-not eat from-it." The woman quotes this command in 3:3 but adds: "and-not you-shall-touch it" — a phrase entirely absent from God's original words. This addition is the woman's own expansion of the prohibition, not something God said. The MT of both the Leningrad Codex and the DSS fragments do not have this phrase in 2:17.

I have translated it exactly as it appears: the woman adds a prohibition God did not state. The rabbinical interpretation (Midrash Rabbah, Avot deRabbi Natan) holds that this addition constituted "making a fence around the Torah" — a protective amplification — but in the narrative it becomes the serpent's leverage point: once the woman says "touch," the serpent can demonstrate touching without death, undermining her confidence in the whole prohibition.

The LXX Vaticanus reproduces the woman's addition faithfully (οὐδὲ μὴ ἅψησθε αὐτοῦ), confirming it is not a scribal insertion but part of the ancient text of Gen 3:3. — EMET

---

### Note 5 — 3:4–5: *Lo Mot Tamutun* — The Negated Emphatic

The serpent's denial in 3:4 mirrors God's warning in 2:17 with a negation. God said: מוֹת תָּמוּת (*mot tamut*, "dying you-shall-die" — infinitive absolute + imperfect = emphatic certainty). The serpent says: לֹא מוֹת תְּמֻתוּן (*lo' mot temutun*, "not dying you-shall-die" — with the plural *temutun* addressing the woman as representative of the couple).

This is a direct negation of the divine emphatic. Not merely "you will not die" (a simple future denial) but the deliberate inversion of YHWH's own emphatic construction. The serpent takes God's most emphatic form of speech and negates it with the same form. I have preserved this: "Not dying you-shall-die."

Note that the serpent shifts from singular (*'at*, "you-f.sg.") in 3:1–3 to plural (*'attem*, implied by *temutun*) in 3:4–5, then back to singular. This shift may address both the woman and the absent man, or it may reflect the couple as a unit. — EMET

---

### Note 6 — 3:5: *Ki Yodea' Elohim* — "For [the] Knowing of God" / "For God Knows"

The Hebrew of 3:5 is syntactically unusual: כִּי יֹדֵעַ אֱלֹהִים (*ki yodea' Elohim*) — literally "for knowing God" or "for [is] God knowing." The participle *yodea'* functions as a predicate: "for God [is one who] knows" or "for God knows." I render it literally as "For [the] knowing of God" to preserve the participial force, though "for God knows" would also be accurate.

The serpent's claim in 3:5 has two clauses: (a) the eyes of both will be opened, and (b) they will be "like God [or: like gods], knowing good and-evil." The phrase כֵּאלֹהִים (*ke-Elohim*) can be rendered "like God" (singular) or "like gods" (plural) — *Elohim* is grammatically plural but used for the singular divine being throughout this narrative. The LXX renders it ὡς θεοί (*hōs theoi*) — "like gods" — taking the plural sense. The Leningrad Codex vocalizes *ke-Elohim* without disambiguation; both "like God" and "like [the] gods" are possible. I render "like God" following the singular divine context, noting the ambiguity.

This verse has been read as the serpent's partial truth: by the end of the chapter (3:22), YHWH God confirms "the man has become like one of us, to-know good and-evil" — the serpent was not entirely wrong. — EMET

---

### Note 7 — 3:6: *Nechmad Ha-'Ets le-Haskil* — "Desirable to-Make-Wise"

The woman's three-part assessment of the tree in 3:6 is a structured triplet: (1) good for food (*tov le-ma'akhal*), (2) a desire to the eyes (*ta'avah hu la-'einayim*), (3) desirable for gaining wisdom (*nechmad ha-'ets le-haskil*). The third phrase contains the verb *sakal* (Hiphil infinitive, "to make wise, to give insight, to cause to understand"). This is a sophisticated, not simply appetitive, desire — the woman wants what the tree offers intellectually/spiritually, not merely physically.

The word *ta'avah* (desire, craving) in clause (2) is the same root as the name of the place in Numbers 11:34 where Israel craved meat in the wilderness — a deliberate later echo. I have preserved the Hebrew verbal texture without smoothing.

The phrase "and-she-gave also to-her-man *with-her* (עִמָּהּ)" is significant. The word *'immah* ("with-her") appears to indicate the man was present throughout. He was not elsewhere; he was *with her*. His silence is his participation. — EMET

---

### Note 8 — 3:7: *Chagorot* (חֲגֹרֹת) — Loin-Coverings / Aprons

After eating, the couple "sewed leaves of fig-tree and-they-made for-themselves *chagorot*." The word חֲגֹרֹת (*chagorot*) comes from *chagar* ("to gird, to bind around the waist") — it refers to a belt, sash, or loin-covering. The traditional rendering "aprons" is a Jacobean English choice (KJV) that does not capture the significance. These are girded coverings for the lower body — precisely the body-part relevant to nakedness/shame in the narrative. I have rendered "loin-coverings [*chagorot*]" to preserve the literal sense while noting the unusual word.

This is the first act of human craft-work in the narrative: making garments. It will be superseded by YHWH God's making of garments of skin in 3:21. The contrast (human fig-leaf sewing vs. divine skin-garment making) is part of the chapter's structure. — EMET

---

### Note 9 — 3:8: *Qol YHWH Elohim Mithallekh ba-Gan* — "The Sound of YHWH God Walking in the Garden"

**Hebrew:** וַיִּשְׁמְעוּ אֶת־קוֹל יְהוָה אֱלֹהִים מִתְהַלֵּךְ בַּגָּן לְרוּחַ הַיּוֹם

This verse contains two exegetical difficulties:

(1) **The subject of *mithallekh* (walking):** The Piel participle *mithallekh* ("walking, going about") agrees grammatically with *qol* (voice/sound) or with YHWH God as the implied subject. Most translations and all major versions understand YHWH God as walking, not the sound itself. I render "the-sound of YHWH God walking" — with the participle attributing the walking to God, and *qol* as the thing that was heard. The LXX Vaticanus renders: τὴν φωνὴν κυρίου τοῦ θεοῦ περιπατοῦντος ἐν τῷ παραδείσῳ — "the voice of the Lord God walking in the garden" — preserving the same construction.

(2) ***Le-ruach ha-yom* (לְרוּחַ הַיּוֹם):** Literally "in-[the]-breeze/wind/spirit of-the-day." The word *ruach* (רוּחַ) means "wind, breath, spirit" — here in the phrase *le-ruach ha-yom* it means the cool evening wind that arises as the day cools. Traditional renderings include "in the cool of the day" (KJV), "in the wind of the day" (NASB margin), "in the breeze of the day" (many modern versions). I render "in-[the]-breeze of-the-day" as the most literal available English phrase. *Le-* here is a locative preposition ("in, at, toward"). The LXX renders τὸ δειλινόν ("toward evening, in the evening") — an interpretive rather than literal rendering that specifies the time of day without translating *ruach*. — EMET

---

### Note 10 — 3:9: *'Ayyekkah* (אַיֶּכָּה) — "Where-Are-You"

YHWH God's first word to the hiding man is the single Hebrew word *'ayyekkah* (אַיֶּכָּה) — a compound of *'ay* (where?) + *ka* (you, 2ms suffix) + the interrogative *heh*. Literally: "Where-are-you?" I have preserved this as a single transliterated word with a bracketed note. The question is not geographical — YHWH God is not confused about the man's location. It is a relational and moral question: the first question God asks of a human being in scripture is "where are you?" — a question about moral situation, relationship, and accountability.

The LXX: ποῦ εἶ (*pou ei*, "where are you?") — standard Greek equivalent, loses the packed single-word Hebrew construction. — EMET

---

### Note 11 — 3:12–13: The Passing of Blame

The man's response deflects in a double movement: "The-woman *whom You-gave* with-me" — implicating both the woman and YHWH God as co-responsible — "she gave to-me from-the-tree." The phrase "whom *You*-gave" (*asher natattah 'immadi*, אֲשֶׁר נָתַתָּה עִמָּדִי) directly attributes the woman's presence to God's action, making God a party to the chain of causation. This is not softened in the translation: I preserve the literal accusation structure.

The woman's deflection is structurally identical but aimed at the serpent: "The-serpent deceived me" (*hisshi'ani*, הִשִּׁיאַנִי) — the Hiphil of *nasha'*, "to deceive, to lead astray, to beguile." The LXX renders *hisshi'ani* as ἠπάτησέν με (*ēpatēsen me*, "deceived/cheated me"), from *apataō*. The NT picks up this exact word in 1 Tim 2:14, using ἐξηπατήθη — "was thoroughly deceived" — with a compound prefix. — EMET

---

### Note 12 — 3:14: *'Arur Attah* — "Cursed You [Are]"

The divine judgments in 3:14–19 contain a crucial structural asymmetry. The serpent is directly cursed: *'arur 'attah* (אָרוּר אַתָּה, "cursed you-[are]") — a predicative adjective applied directly to the serpent itself. The ground is also cursed: *'arurah ha-'adamah* (3:17, "cursed [is] the-ground"). But the *woman* and the *man* are not directly cursed. They receive descriptions of painful consequences, but the curse-word *'arur* is not applied to them. This distinction is intentional and significant: the text curses the serpent and the earth, but not the humans. I have preserved this asymmetry precisely in the translation by reserving the word "cursed" for 3:14 and 3:17 exactly where *'arur* appears.

The serpent's curse has two elements: (a) physical posture — "on your-belly you-shall-go" — and (b) diet — "and-dust you-shall-eat." Neither is presented as a change from a previous state in the plain sense of the MT (the serpent is not said to have had legs before). The curse is the *prescribing* of a mode of existence as a mark of degradation. — EMET

---

### Note 13 — 3:15: *'Eyvah* and *Teshuphenu* — Enmity and the Bruising

**Hebrew (3:15):** וְאֵיבָה אָשִׁית בֵּינְךָ וּבֵין הָאִשָּׁה וּבֵין זַרְעֲךָ וּבֵין זַרְעָהּ הוּא יְשׁוּפְךָ רֹאשׁ וְאַתָּה תְּשׁוּפֶנּוּ עָקֵב

This verse is the most theologically contested in the chapter, known in Christian tradition as the *Protoevangelium* ("first gospel") — the initial promise of redemption. I translate it without prejudicing that interpretation.

Key terms:

**'Eyvah (אֵיבָה) — "enmity, hostility":** A noun from the root *'ayav* ("to be hostile to"). I render "enmity" following standard lexical convention.

**Zar'akha / zar'ah (זַרְעֲךָ / זַרְעָהּ) — "your-seed / her-seed":** The word *zera'* ("seed") is grammatically singular in Hebrew but can be collective ("offspring"). It is the same word used in the Abrahamic promises (Gen 12:7, 13:15, etc.) and in Gal 3:16 where Paul argues from the singular *zera'*. I render literally "your-seed" and "her-seed."

**Hu yeshupekha ro'sh / ve-'attah teshuphenu 'aqev:** The key verb *shuph* (שׁוּף) is extremely rare — it appears only three times in the Hebrew Bible (here twice, and Job 9:17). Its meaning is debated: "to strike, to bruise, to crush, to lie in wait for, to snap at." The same verb (*yeshupekha* / *teshuphenu*) is used for both the seed-of-the-woman striking the serpent's head AND the serpent striking the seed's heel. Both parties do the same action to different body parts: head vs. heel.

The Vulgate (Jerome's Latin) translates *shuph* as *conteret* ("she will crush") for the first occurrence and *insidiaberis* ("you will lie in wait for") for the second — creating an asymmetry the Hebrew does not support. This Vulgate asymmetry fed the Christian *Protoevangelium* reading. The MT uses the same verb for both actions.

The pronoun *hu'* (הוּא, "he") before *yeshupekha* is masculine singular — "he will-bruise." The referent is grammatically the masculine *zera'* ("seed") — the singular collective offspring of the woman. The LXX renders *hu'* as αὐτός (*autos*, "he") — masculine — in Vaticanus, against the Vetus Latina tradition which has *ipsa* ("she"), feeding the Marian reading. The Vaticanus LXX confirms the masculine pronoun.

I translate *shuph* as "bruise/strike" throughout, applying the same English word to both occurrences to preserve the Hebrew symmetry. — EMET

---

### Note 14 — 3:16: *'Itsabon* and *Ittsavonek* — Pain in Childbearing

**Hebrew (3:16):** הַרְבָּה אַרְבֶּה עִצְּבוֹנֵךְ וְהֵרֹנֵךְ בְּעֶצֶב תֵּלְדִי בָנִים

The verse opens with another infinitive absolute + imperfect emphatic construction: *harbah 'arbeh* — "multiplying I-will-multiply" (cf. the emphatic *mot tamut* in 2:17 and its echo in 3:4). I preserve the doubling literally.

The word עִצְּבוֹן (*'itsabon*) is related to *'etsev* ("pain, sorrow, toil") — the same root as *'itsavon* in 3:17 applied to the man's labor. The same pain-word applies to both the woman (3:16) and the man (3:17), creating a structural parallel: both bear *'itsabon* — she in childbearing, he in agriculture. The traditional rendering "sorrow" (KJV) understates the physical labor-pain dimension. I render "pain" throughout.

The phrase וְהֵרֹנֵךְ (*ve-heronekh*, "and-your-conception") is the noun *herayon* ("pregnancy, conception"), here in construct with the 2fs suffix. The MT vocalization in the Leningrad Codex is clearly pointing to "your-conception/pregnancy" as a second object of the multiplied pain. Traditional renderings frequently smooth this into "your childbearing" (collapsing conception and birth), but the MT distinguishes them: both the process of conception and the process of birthing will involve increased *'itsabon*.

**And-to your-man [shall be] your-desire, and-he shall-rule over-you:** The Hebrew תְּשׁוּקָתֵךְ (*teshukhatek*, "your-desire") is from *shuq* — the same root used for the man's desire toward the woman in Song of Songs 7:11. The LXX renders ἀποστροφή σου (*apostrophē sou*, "your turning/return") — a significantly different rendering that has "your turning will be toward your husband." The Vaticanus LXX text is clear on this variant: ἀποστροφή rather than ἐπιθυμία ("desire"). The Vaticanus reading of ἀποστροφή ("turning") has been the basis of a competing interpretive tradition; the MT *teshukah* clearly means "desire/longing," not simply "turning."

The same word *teshukah* appears in Gen 4:7 for sin's desire toward Cain. — EMET

---

### Note 15 — 3:17: *Be-'avurekha* — "On-Your-Account" / "Because of You"

The phrase בַּעֲבוּרֶךָ (*ba-'avurekha*) in the curse on the ground (3:17) means "on account of you, for your sake, because of you." I render "on-your-account" to preserve the direct causal attribution. The ground is cursed not because of its own fault but as a consequence of Adam's action. This preposition carries moral weight.

The curse on the man in 3:17–19 mirrors the curse on the serpent in 3:14 structurally:
- Serpent: cursed / on your belly / dust you shall eat / all the days of your life
- Man: cursed [is the ground] / in toil you shall eat / [bread] / all the days of your life / until your return to dust

The parallelism is deliberate. The serpent eats dust as its degradation; the man returns to dust as his mortality. — EMET

---

### Note 16 — 3:19: *Be-Ze'at Appekha* — "By the Sweat of Your Nostrils"

The Hebrew בְּזֵעַת אַפֶּיךָ (*be-ze'at appekha*) is literally "in/by [the]-sweat of your-nostrils." The word אַף (*'af*, with the dual suffix *appekha* = "your nostrils/face") is the standard Hebrew for "face" by extension but literally means "nostril" — the prominent feature of the face. I preserve "nostrils" as the literal rendering, noting in brackets [face] as a traditional gloss, because the literal body-part image of sweat dripping from the nose/face during heavy labor is precisely what the text evokes. The LXX renders τῷ ἱδρῶτι τοῦ προσώπου σου ("by the sweat of your face"), using *prosōpon* ("face") — the conventional Greek idiom. The MT "nostrils" is more visceral. — EMET

---

### Note 17 — 3:20: *Chavvah* (חַוָּה) — The Naming of Eve

**Hebrew:** וַיִּקְרָא הָאָדָם שֵׁם אִשְׁתּוֹ חַוָּה כִּי הִוא הָיְתָה אֵם כָּל־חָי

The name חַוָּה (*Chavvah*) — traditionally "Eve" in English — is etymologically connected to *chai* (חַי, "living") and the verb *chayah* ("to live"). The man's naming explanation is: "for she was [the] mother of all *chai* (living)." The wordplay between *Chavvah* and *chai* is visible in the Hebrew and I have preserved it by writing "Chavvah [Eve]" in the translation.

This naming takes place *after* the judgment — not in the garden of innocence but in the aftermath of the fall. This is the second time the man names the woman: in 2:23 he called her *'ishshah* ("woman") as a class; here he gives her a personal name. The name is oriented toward life, not death — despite the death-sentence of 3:19. The LXX renders Χωά (*Chōa*) — a Greek transliteration of *Chavvah*.

The narrative places the naming at this point, after the divine judgments but before the expulsion, as a moment of faith: the man names his wife "life" even as they face mortality. Whether this is meant as an act of faith or is simply etiological narration, the structure is significant. — EMET

---

### Note 18 — 3:21: *Kothonot 'Or* — "Garments of Skin"

**Hebrew:** וַיַּעַשׂ יְהוָה אֱלֹהִים לְאָדָם וּלְאִשְׁתּוֹ כָּתְנוֹת עוֹר וַיַּלְבִּשֵׁם

The noun כָּתְנוֹת (*kothonot*, "garments, tunics") refers to a close-fitting undergarment — the same word for Joseph's tunic (*ketonet passim*, "tunic of stripes/colors") in Gen 37:3 and the garment of the high priest (Exod 28:4). These are not outer robes but fitted undergarments — significant coverage, replacing the couple's fig-leaf *chagorot*.

The crucial word is עוֹר (*'or*, "skin"). The Leningrad Codex writes it עוֹר with *ayin* — "skin, hide, leather." A well-known ancient variant in some manuscripts and in rabbinic tradition writes אוֹר with *aleph* — "light." This gives "garments of light," and is supported by some early rabbinic sources (cf. Genesis Rabbah 20:12 which discusses both readings). The *ayin/aleph* interchange is a known scribal phenomenon in Hebrew. The Leningrad Codex clearly reads עוֹר ("skin"), and I translate accordingly. The *'or* ("light") reading is a significant textual variant that I note here but do not adopt.

The LXX renders δερματίνους χιτῶνας (*dermatinoùs chitōnas*, "leather/skin tunics") — confirming the *ayin*-spelling "skin" tradition, not "light." The Vaticanus LXX is unambiguous on this point. — EMET

---

### Note 19 — 3:22: The Broken Sentence — *Anapodoton*

**Hebrew (3:22):** וְעַתָּה פֶּן־יִשְׁלַח יָדוֹ וְלָקַח גַּם מֵעֵץ הַחַיִּים וְאָכַל וָחַי לְעֹלָם

The sentence beginning with "And-now, lest he-stretch-out his-hand..." trails off without a main clause completing it. YHWH God does not finish the sentence — or more precisely, the action of 3:23 ("And-sent-him-out YHWH God from the-garden") follows immediately as if carrying out the implied but unstated conclusion. This grammatical break — an *anapodoton* (a sentence with a suspended conditional and no expressed consequence) — is present in the MT and is not a scribal error. It reflects a divine urgency: the sentence is broken off by action.

I have preserved the broken syntax with an em-dash at the end of 3:22. Smoothing it into a complete sentence (as many translations do) erases one of the most dramatically significant grammatical features of the passage.

The phrase כְּאַחַד מִמֶּנּוּ (*ke-'echad mimmenu*, "like one of-us") in 3:22 uses the same divine plural as 1:26 ("let us make man") and 11:7 ("let us go down"). The LXX renders ὡς εἷς ἐξ ἡμῶν (*hōs heis ex hēmōn*, "like one of us") — confirming the plural. — EMET

---

### Note 20 — 3:24: *Keruvim* (כְּרוּבִים) — The Cherubim

**Hebrew:** וַיַּשְׁכֵּן מִקֶּדֶם לְגַן־עֵדֶן אֶת־הַכְּרֻבִים וְאֵת לַהַט הַחֶרֶב הַמִּתְהַפֶּכֶת

The *keruvim* (כְּרוּבִים, singular *keruv*) are placed *east* of the garden — echoing *miqqedem* ("from-east") of 2:8. The garden was planted *from-east* (or *of-old*); the guards are placed *miqqedem* (east). The man is expelled to the east of Eden, and the path back is blocked on the east. This reversal is spatial and symbolic.

The Hebrew מִקֶּדֶם (*miqqedem*) in 3:24 should be rendered "from-east" (locative), i.e., the cherubim were stationed at the eastern approach to the garden. This is the standard interpretation and is supported by the LXX: κατέναντι τοῦ παραδείσου τῆς τρυφῆς ("opposite/in-front-of the garden of pleasure").

**The *keruvim*:** These are not the chubby winged infants of later European art. In the Hebrew Bible, *keruvim* are formidable composite creatures — associated with the divine throne (Ps 18:10; Ezek 1, 10), the Ark of the Covenant (Exod 25:18–22), and the Temple decorations (1 Kgs 6:23–28). Their function here is as guardians of sacred space — the exact function they serve flanking the Ark.

**לַהַט הַחֶרֶב הַמִּתְהַפֶּכֶת (*lahat ha-cherev ha-mithappekhet*):** "the-flame of the-sword the-revolving." The word *lahat* means "flame" or "flaming" (the *lahat* of the fire, Ps 29:7; Joel 1:19). *Ha-mithappekhet* is the Hitpael participle of *haphak* ("to turn, to overturn, to transform") — meaning "the turning/revolving/whirling [one]." This is the whirling/flaming sword, continuously revolving, blocking all approach. I have preserved both "flame" (not merely "flaming") and "revolving" (not merely "flashing") as the most literal rendering.

The LXX renders: καὶ τὴν φλογίνην ῥομφαίαν τὴν στρεφομένην (*kai tēn phloginēn rhomphaian tēn strephomenēn*) — "and the fiery sword the-turning/revolving." Vaticanus confirms both the fire and the revolving motion. — EMET

---

### Note 21 — 3:24: *Lishmar 'Et Derekh 'Ets ha-Chayyim* — "To-Guard [the]-Way of [the]-Tree of-the-Life"

The final phrase of Genesis 3 is *lishmar 'et derekh 'ets ha-chayyim* — "to-guard the-way of the-tree of-the-life." The *derekh* (דֶּרֶךְ, "way, path, road") before *'ets ha-chayyim* ("tree of-the-life") suggests that what is being guarded is not the tree itself but the *access-path* to it — the route by which a human could reach and eat from it. This is the *derekh* as the approach-road, not the tree as a stationary object.

The article on *ha-chayyim* ("the-life" = "of life") is present in the MT (as in 2:9 and 3:22). I preserve it as "tree of-the-life" — the definite article indicating the specific, unique tree of life, not a generic tree of life.

Genesis 3 ends with closed access to the tree of life — a motif that resonates across the canon from Proverbs 3:18 ("she [wisdom] is a tree of life to those who grasp her") to Revelation 22:2, 14, 19 (the tree of life restored in the new Jerusalem). The EMET translation does not impose these connections but notes them as the literary frame within which this closing phrase operates. — EMET

### Note 22 — DSS Evidence: 4QGen-b

The Dead Sea Scrolls fragment 4QGen-b (4Q2) preserves portions of Genesis 3. The surviving fragments, as documented in DJD XII (Ulrich et al.), provide evidence for portions of the chapter — including fragments touching Genesis 3:1–2 and scattered verses. The 4QGen-b fragments consistently align with the Masoretic text tradition (consonantal text), without significant variants that would affect the translation above.

Specifically: the serpent's opening speech (3:1–5), the eating scene (3:6), and portions of the divine judgments (3:14ff.) are represented in 4QGen-b fragments where they survive. The text of 4QGen-b is written in a Herodian hand (1st century BCE to 1st century CE) using the standard Jewish square script. Where the fragments overlap with the Leningrad Codex, they agree on the consonantal text.

No orthographic variants in 4QGen-b for Genesis 3 are of sufficient significance to require a note on the translation above. The DSS, for this chapter as for Genesis 2, function as a confirming witness to the proto-MT tradition, adding approximately 1,000 years of depth. — EMET

---

### Note 23 — LXX Vaticanus: Summary of Significant Variants for Genesis 3

The Codex Vaticanus page examined (GEN_003_vaticanus_page_001.jpg — Vat.gr.1209, labeled "CAP. III" at the top) presents the LXX text of Genesis 3 in three columns of uncial Greek, written without word division (*scriptio continua*) and without accents/breathings in the original hand. The page is legible with careful attention. The following variants are of translational significance:

**3:1 — *'af ki* → ὅτι ("because/that"):** See Note 2 above. The LXX flattens the rhetorical force of *'af ki*.

**3:1 — *'arum* → φρονιμώτατος (*phronimōtatos*, "most prudent/shrewd"):** The LXX renders the comparative force with a superlative: "the most shrewd/prudent of all the beasts." The MT's *'arum mikkol* is comparative ("more shrewd than all"), not superlative. The LXX over-intensifies slightly, but the semantic range is preserved. Importantly, the LXX's *phronimos* ("prudent, shrewd, wise") for *'arum* is the word used in NT wisdom contexts (cf. Matt 10:16, "be *phronimoi* as serpents") — a direct literary echo of this LXX translation choice.

**3:5 — *ke-Elohim* → ὡς θεοί ("like gods"):** As noted in Note 6, the LXX reads the plural, while the MT is ambiguous. The Vaticanus reading is confirmed: ὡς θεοί (plural). — EMET

**3:8 — *le-ruach ha-yom* → τὸ δειλινόν ("in the evening"):** The LXX interprets the phrase as a time reference ("toward evening") rather than translating *ruach* ("wind/breeze"). The Vaticanus text is clear. See Note 9.

**3:14–15 — LXX closely follows MT** with no significant structural variants in the curse on the serpent. The pronoun *hu'* in 3:15 is αὐτός (masculine) in Vaticanus — against the Vetus Latina *ipsa* (feminine). This is the most important textual witness confirming the masculine reading.

**3:16 — *teshukhatek* → ἀποστροφή σου ("your turning"):** A significant variant. The MT *teshukah* means "desire/longing." The LXX *apostrophē* means "turning/returning." This is not a minor lexical variant: the LXX's choice interprets the woman's relationship to her husband as a kind of turning/returning rather than desire. The Vaticanus reading of *apostrophē* is unambiguous on the manuscript page. Later LXX manuscripts have ἐπιθυμία ("desire"), harmonizing to the MT; but Vaticanus preserves the distinctive *apostrophē* reading.

**3:19 — *appekha* → τοῦ προσώπου σου ("of your face"):** LXX renders "nostrils" as "face." See Note 16.

**3:21 — *kothonot 'or* → δερματίνους χιτῶνας ("leather/skin tunics"):** LXX confirms the *ayin*-reading "skin," not "light." See Note 18.

**3:22 — *ke-'echad mimmenu* → ὡς εἷς ἐξ ἡμῶν ("like one of us"):** LXX confirms the divine plural. The broken sentence structure of 3:22 (Note 19) is preserved in the LXX as well — the sentence trails off without completion, and the LXX's Greek reflects the same *anapodoton*. — EMET

---

### Note 24 — Petuchah and Setumah Markers (Parashah Divisions)

In the Leningrad Codex, as visible in the manuscript images, the Masoretic paragraph markers regulate the reading of the text. Genesis 3 does not begin with a full *petuchah* (open paragraph) break from the end of Genesis 2:25 in most manuscripts — the *'arummim*/*'arum* wordplay links the two units deliberately. The chapter as a structural unit is a later canonical division.

Within Genesis 3, Masoretic *setumah* (closed paragraph) or *petuchah* markers appear at points including after 3:13 (before the divine judgments) and after 3:19 (before the naming of Eve), marking the structural movements of the chapter: the temptation (3:1–7), the confrontation (3:8–13), the judgments (3:14–19), and the aftermath (3:20–24). The precise positions of these markers in the Leningrad Codex are visible in the manuscript images through the spacing conventions between columns. — EMET
